Early adopters expand their leadership in Enterprise AI, while a large minority stands.
About 42% of companies with more than 1,000 employees run AI in production, and 59% of companies that already use or research AI have accelerated their investments in the last 24 months.
However, another 40% remain stuck in the experiment and cannot put their models into use.

The research group has the same obstacles: lack of AI skills (33%), excessive data complexity (25%) and ethical concerns (23%), followed by projects that are difficult to integrate and scale (22%) and high costs (21%).
Especially for generative AI, data protection (57%) and trust and transparency (43%) dominate as inhibitors.

Cheaper and simpler tool drives the assumption in the other direction: more accessible AI tools (45%), pressure to reduce costs and automation (42%) and AI in standard business software (37%) lead the drivers.
Active use is highest in India, the UAE, Singapore and China as well as within financial services.
Talent remains a brake - one of five companies can't do the skills they need, and only 34% are retraining their people.

Companies that want to join the providing majority should invest in skills, as reskilling and R & D rank are the top investments, reduce their data complexity and rely on accessible, embedded tools to shorten the way to production.

They should also put trusted AI intentions into practice - reduce distortions, track data origin and ensure explainability - because 85% agree that customers prefer transparent AI, but have traded less than half.
The removal of these barriers is the difference between experimenting with AI and the benefit of it.
